name: Myopia
summary: Eye too long / power too strong → image focuses in front of the retina → distance blur. High myopia is a structural risk factor for retinal tears and glaucoma later.

name: Hyperopia
summary: Eye too short / power too weak → image focuses behind the retina. Young patients can hide it by accommodating; cycloplegic refraction reveals the true magnitude.

name: Astigmatism
summary: Cornea (or lens) curvature differs by meridian → light focuses at multiple points → smearing at all distances.

name: Presbyopia
summary: The lens stiffens with age; ciliary muscle can no longer change its shape enough to focus near. A clock, not a disease.

name: Cataract
summary: The crystalline lens clouds with age. Gradual painless blur, glare at night. Treated by surgical lens removal + IOL implant; biometry drives IOL choice.

name: Dry age-related macular degeneration
nameVi: AMD thể khô (thoái hoá hoàng điểm tuổi già)
summary: RPE support cells attrit; drusen accumulate; photoreceptors slowly starve. Late stage = geographic atrophy. Gradual central blur in the elderly.

name: Wet age-related macular degeneration
summary: Choroidal neovascularization breaks through Bruch's membrane → leak / bleed → sudden central distortion (metamorphopsia). Treated with regular intravitreal anti-VEGF.

name: Diabetic macular edema
nameVi: Phù hoàng điểm do tiểu đường
summary: Capillary leakage in a diabetic causes fluid accumulation in the macula → central blur. Treated with anti-VEGF ± focal laser; tighter sugar control.

name: Rhegmatogenous retinal detachment
summary: A retinal tear admits fluid behind the neuroretina, which peels off the RPE. Flashes → shower of floaters → curtain. Surgical urgency.

name: PVD with retinal tear
nameVi: Bong dịch kính sau có rách võng mạc
summary: Posterior vitreous detachment can tug a retinal tear. Tear without detachment is treatable with laser; missing it leads to a full RD.

name: Posterior vitreous detachment
summary: The vitreous gel pulls away from the retina. Usually harmless once a tear is ruled out, but the symptom pattern (new floaters/flashes) is identical to RD onset.

name: Macular hole
summary: A full-thickness defect at the foveal centre. Central distortion / blur. Closed surgically with vitrectomy + ILM peel + gas.

name: Epiretinal membrane
summary: A wrinkly membrane on the macular surface. Distortion ± mild blur. Observe if mild; peel surgically if vision-limiting.

name: Retinitis pigmentosa
summary: Inherited photoreceptor degeneration starting peripherally. Night blindness → tunnel vision. No general cure; gene therapy emerging for specific genotypes.

name: Primary open-angle glaucoma
summary: Silent IOP rise (open angle, clogged trabecular meshwork) → progressive optic-nerve damage → peripheral field loss first. Asymptomatic until late.

name: Acute angle-closure glaucoma
summary: Iris suddenly covers the drainage angle → IOP rockets. Sudden severe pain, red eye, halos, nausea, rock-hard globe, mid-dilated fixed pupil.

name: Normal-tension glaucoma
summary: Glaucomatous optic-nerve damage despite IOP within the statistical normal range. Vascular factors suspected. Treated by aggressive IOP lowering anyway.

name: Ocular hypertension
summary: IOP above 21 with a healthy disc and visual field. Monitor; treat if risk factors are high.

name: Neovascular glaucoma
summary: Ischemia (DR, RVO, CRAO) drives VEGF → new vessels grow in the angle and block drainage. Painful, hard to control, often follows the original ischemia by months.

name: Optic neuritis
summary: Inflammation of the optic nerve (often demyelinating, MS-associated). Subacute one-eye blur + pain on eye movement + colour desaturation + RAPD.

name: Non-arteritic anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy
nameVi: Bệnh thần kinh thị thiếu máu trước không do viêm động mạch (NAION)
summary: Infarct of the optic-nerve-head circulation. Sudden painless altitudinal field loss. Manage vascular risk factors; no proven specific treatment.

name: Giant cell arteritis (arteritic AION)
nameVi: Viêm động mạch tế bào khổng lồ
summary: Granulomatous arteritis of medium vessels. Sudden vision loss + headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, polymyalgia in age 50+. Untreated, blinds the other eye within days.

name: Papilledema
summary: Bilateral disc swelling from raised intracranial pressure. Brief visual obscurations + headache. Implies a mass, hydrocephalus, sinus thrombosis, or IIH.

name: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension
nameVi: Tăng áp lực nội sọ vô căn
summary: Raised ICP without a mass — classically young, overweight women. Papilledema → progressive field loss if untreated.

name: Chiasmal compression (pituitary tumour)
nameVi: Chèn ép giao thoa (u tuyến yên)
summary: Mass at the chiasm compresses the crossing nasal fibres → bitemporal hemianopia. Often slow, missed for years.

name: Occipital lobe stroke
summary: Homonymous field loss (same side missing in both eyes) from a posterior cerebral artery infarct.

name: Strabismus (adult)
summary: An eye that turns; double vision until the brain suppresses one image. May be longstanding decompensated childhood strabismus or new (rule out neuro cause).

name: Amblyopia (lazy eye)
summary: Cortical suppression of one eye that received a blurry or misaligned image during childhood. Treatable only inside the critical window (~age 7–8).

name: CN III palsy — compressive
nameVi: Liệt dây III do chèn ép
summary: External compression of CN III (classically a posterior communicating artery aneurysm) — diplopia + ptosis + down-and-out eye + dilated unresponsive pupil.

name: CN III palsy — ischemic (pupil-sparing)
nameVi: Liệt dây III do thiếu máu (đồng tử không bị)
summary: Microvascular CN III palsy in a diabetic / hypertensive patient — diplopia + ptosis with a normally reactive pupil. Often self-resolves over weeks.

name: CN IV (trochlear) palsy
summary: Vertical / torsional diplopia worse on looking down (reading, stairs) + a compensatory head tilt away from the affected side.

name: CN VI (abducens) palsy
summary: Horizontal diplopia, the affected eye cannot abduct. Microvascular in adults; in children think raised ICP / mass.

name: Evaporative dry eye / MGD
nameVi: Khô mắt thể bay hơi / rối loạn tuyến Meibomian
summary: Clogged meibomian glands → thinner lipid layer → faster evaporation → fluctuating blur (clears with blink), grit, burning. Paradoxical tearing common.

name: Aqueous-deficient dry eye
summary: Lacrimal gland under-produces aqueous (e.g. Sjögren's). Severe dryness, often bilateral with dry mouth. Workup for autoimmune cause.

name: Blepharitis
summary: Inflammation of the lid margin (anterior — staphylococcal/seborrheic; posterior — MGD). Crusty, itchy, red lid margins.

name: Viral conjunctivitis
summary: Highly contagious. Watery discharge, follicular reaction, often started in one eye then the other. Self-limited over 1-2 weeks.

name: Bacterial conjunctivitis
nameVi: Viêm kết mạc do vi khuẩn
summary: Purulent (sticky) discharge, eyes glued shut in the morning. Often self-limited; topical antibiotic shortens course.

name: Allergic conjunctivitis
summary: Itchy, watery, bilateral. Triggered by allergens (pollen, dust, pet). Itch is the cardinal symptom.

name: Bacterial keratitis (corneal ulcer)
nameVi: Loét giác mạc do vi khuẩn
summary: Pain + red + photophobia + a white corneal infiltrate. Most cases follow contact-lens overwear. Can perforate the cornea within days.

name: Herpes simplex keratitis
summary: Branching (dendritic) corneal ulcer. Recurrent. Never give topical steroid alone — it makes it worse.

name: Acanthamoeba keratitis
summary: Devastating in CL wearers who rinse cases with tap water. Disproportionate pain. Ring infiltrate is classic but late.

name: Corneal abrasion
summary: A scratch on the corneal epithelium. Sudden sharp pain, foreign-body sensation, tearing. Heals in days; antibiotic prophylaxis.

name: Keratoconus
summary: Progressive corneal thinning and steepening. Young patients with worsening astigmatism. CXL halts progression; rigid / scleral CL or PK for advanced disease.

name: Recurrent corneal erosion
nameVi: Loét giác mạc tái phát (trợt biểu mô)
summary: The corneal epithelium fails to re-adhere to its basement membrane (often after an old abrasion or in epithelial basement membrane dystrophy). Classic pattern: sharp pain on awakening that subsides during the day; episodic, recurrent over months-years.

name: Pterygium
summary: Fibrovascular growth from the conjunctiva onto the cornea. UV exposure–related. Excise if visually threatening or chronically irritated.

name: Non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy
nameVi: Bệnh võng mạc đái tháo đường chưa tăng sinh
summary: Microaneurysms, dot/blot haemorrhages, exudates. Often asymptomatic until DME develops. ETDRS-graded.

name: Proliferative diabetic retinopathy
nameVi: Bệnh võng mạc đái tháo đường tăng sinh
summary: Ischemia drives neovascularization → vitreous haemorrhage, tractional detachment, neovascular glaucoma. Floaters, sudden loss in haemorrhage.

name: Hypertensive retinopathy
nameVi: Bệnh võng mạc do tăng huyết áp
summary: Arteriolar narrowing, AV nicking, flame haemorrhages, cotton-wool spots. Usually a chronic-BP finding; in malignant HTN the disc swells.

name: Retinal vein occlusion
summary: Branch or central retinal vein clot → 'blood and thunder' fundus, macular edema. Painless, often sudden. Treat the edema with anti-VEGF / steroid; PRP for ischemia.

name: Central retinal artery occlusion
nameVi: Tắc động mạch trung tâm võng mạc
summary: Embolic occlusion of the central retinal artery — 'eye stroke'. Sudden painless severe vision loss; cherry-red spot. Manage like a brain stroke.

name: Retinopathy of prematurity
nameVi: Bệnh võng mạc trẻ sinh non
summary: Abnormal retinal vessel growth in premature babies. Caught on newborn dilated screening. Treat sight-threatening stages with anti-VEGF or laser.

name: Anterior uveitis (iritis)
summary: Inflammation of the iris / ciliary body. Pain, photophobia, circumcorneal redness, small pupil, cells and flare. Often HLA-B27 / spondyloarthritis associated.

name: Posterior / intermediate uveitis
nameVi: Viêm màng bồ đào sau / trung gian
summary: Inflammation of the choroid / vitreous. Floaters, blur. Look for sarcoid, TB, toxoplasmosis, syphilis, Behcet, CMV.

name: Scleritis
summary: Deep boring eye pain that wakes the patient, tender to touch, violet hue. Often a sign of systemic autoimmune disease (RA, vasculitis).

name: Episcleritis
summary: Self-limited inflammation of the episclera. Mild redness/ache, sectoral. Distinguished from scleritis by lack of deep boring pain.

name: Endophthalmitis
summary: Infection inside the eye — post-op, post-injection, or traumatic. Pain + vision drop + hypopyon. Hours matter; vision often permanently lost.

name: Uveitic glaucoma (inflammatory secondary glaucoma)
nameVi: Glôcôm thứ phát do viêm màng bồ đào
summary: Secondary IOP rise in the setting of active or chronic anterior uveitis. Mechanisms: trabeculitis (inflammatory cells blocking outflow), steroid response from chronic topical steroid use, posterior synechiae causing pupillary block, or peripheral anterior synechiae. Recurrent uveitis + elevated IOP. Treat the inflammation AND the pressure simultaneously.

name: Horner syndrome
summary: Sympathetic chain interruption → small pupil + ptosis + anhidrosis on one side. New + neck pain = carotid dissection; isolated = workup for cause.

name: Adie's tonic pupil
summary: Damaged ciliary ganglion → one large pupil that reacts slowly to near, barely to light. Benign in isolation.

name: Chemical burn (acid / alkali)
summary: Acid burns coagulate (less penetration); alkali burns saponify and penetrate deeply (much worse). Irrigate first, ask questions later.

name: Open globe injury
summary: Globe laceration or rupture. Misshapen pupil, low IOP, prolapsed uveal tissue, very low VA. Shield and surgery; never measure IOP or instill drops.

name: Hyphema (blood in anterior chamber)
summary: Blood layer behind the cornea after blunt trauma. Watch for rebleed (day 3–5) and IOP spike; sickle-cell patients are at much higher risk.

name: Retinoblastoma
summary: Paediatric intraocular cancer. White pupillary reflex (leukocoria) or strabismus in a young child. Curable when caught early.

name: Congenital cataract
nameVi: Đục thuỷ tinh thể bẩm sinh
summary: Lens opacity present at birth or early childhood. Can also cause leukocoria. Surgery + amblyopia management within the critical window.

name: Orbital cellulitis
summary: Bacterial infection behind the orbital septum, often spreading from ethmoid sinusitis. Periorbital swelling + fever + proptosis + pain on eye movement + restricted motility. Can extend intracranially (cavernous sinus thrombosis, abscess). Paediatric emergency.

checkFlags: [CHECK] preseptal vs orbital distinction depends on motility + proptosis; clinical reviewer should confirm thresholds

name: Hordeolum (stye)
summary: Acute bacterial infection of an eyelash follicle (external hordeolum) or a meibomian gland (internal). Painful tender red lump on the lid margin; usually staphylococcal. Most resolve in 1-2 weeks with warm compresses; rarely needs incision.

name: Chalazion
summary: Chronic granulomatous inflammation of a meibomian gland after duct obstruction. Painless firm subcutaneous nodule, often weeks to months. Frequently follows an unresolved hordeolum. Treat with warm compresses; incision + curettage or intralesional steroid for persistent lesions.

name: Eyelid myokymia
summary: Benign involuntary contraction of orbicularis oculi fibers, classically the lower lid. Triggers: fatigue, stress, caffeine, sleep deprivation, eye strain. Self-limited over days-weeks. Reassurance is the treatment.

name: Migraine with aura
nameVi: Đau nửa đầu có hào quang thị giác
summary: Reversible visual aura (classically zigzag scintillating scotoma, fortification spectra, or bilateral flashes lasting 15-60 minutes) preceding or accompanying a unilateral throbbing headache, often with nausea or photophobia. Recurrent in a young-to-middle-aged patient with a personal or family history. NOT a retinal detachment despite the flashes — bilateral involvement and the temporal pattern distinguish.

name: Asthenopia / digital eye strain
nameVi: Mỏi mắt do nhìn gần / hội chứng mắt do dùng màn hình
summary: Eye fatigue from prolonged near work, screen use, or uncorrected refractive error / convergence insufficiency. Frontal headache, blur clearing with rest, gritty / dry sensation, intermittent diplopia at near. Often coexists with MGD-style dry eye.

name: Coats disease / other paediatric retinal cause of leukocoria
nameVi: Bệnh Coats / nguyên nhân võng mạc trẻ em
summary: Telangiectatic retinal vessels in a child, often unilateral, presenting with leukocoria or strabismus. Distinguished from retinoblastoma on imaging.

name: Central serous retinopathy
nameVi: Bệnh thấm thấu huyết tương dưới loạn
summary: Serous detachment of the neurosensory retina at the macula, typically in young-to-middle-aged men under stress or on corticosteroids. Presents with acute-to-subacute central blur, metamorphopsia, micropsia, and a central scotoma. OCT shows subretinal fluid without retinal hemorrhage or drusen, distinguishing it from wet-AMD which occurs in older patients with hemorrhage and macular drusen.

name: Fuchs endothelial corneal dystrophy
nameVi: Thoái hoá nội mô giác mạc Fuchs
summary: Progressive endothelial cell loss leads to stromal corneal edema, worse on waking (when the pump is weakest) and improving through the day as the eye dries. Bilateral gradual blur, glare, and halos. Guttata visible on slit-lamp exam. Late stage may require corneal transplant.

name: Nasolacrimal duct obstruction (NLDO)
summary: The nasolacrimal duct (carrying tears from the eye to the nose) is partially or completely blocked, causing constant tearing (epiphora), watery discharge, and sometimes tender medial-canthal swelling (dacryocystitis or mucocele). Common in infants (congenital, often self-resolve by 12 months) and older adults (acquired obstruction). Conservative management with lid hygiene and warm compresses; probing in infants or dacryocystorhinostomy if chronic.

name: Strabismus (childhood, esotropia or exotropia)
nameVi: Lác mắt trẻ em (vào trong hoặc vào ngoài)
summary: Manifest eye turn (eso- or exotropia) detected in a child. Children suppress the misaligned eye's image (unlike adults who report diplopia), creating amblyopia risk. Treatable during the critical window (~age 0–8) with spectacles, prism, patching, or muscle surgery.

name: Exposure keratopathy
nameVi: Bệnh giác mạc do tiếp xúc (khô do bất toàn đóng mi)
summary: Incomplete eyelid closure (lagophthalmos) from facial nerve palsy (CN VII), severe proptosis, or post-blepharoplasty → inferior corneal drying and epithelial breakdown. Presents with gritty/burning inferior eye, foreign-body sensation, and risk of infectious keratitis or corneal scarring if untreated.
